Minimal Sliding Glass Doors for Contemporary Designs

Every Himax X Sliding Door is tailored to its architectural context—crafted from the highest quality materials and finished to exacting standards. With options for motorisation, pocket integration, and open corners, it is as adaptable as it is striking.
The Himax X Sliding Door system is a seamless blend of innovation, clarity and architectural elegance.

Designed to push the boundaries of minimalist design, it delivers floor-to-ceiling glass with ultra-slim 21mm sightlines and hidden thresholds, creating uninterrupted transitions between interior and exterior. With manual or motorised operation, and support for panels up to 5m high and 1000kg. Whether framing a view or defining a boundary, this system elevates the experience of space and light.
Expanding spaces both physically and visually.
Its discreet profiles, concealed thresholds, and dynamic configuration options make it a powerful design tool—capable of expanding spaces both physically and visually.

The Himax X Sliding Door is crafted for ambitious architectural schemes that demand visual lightness and structural strength. Whether applied to residential or commercial settings, it allows for wide, open expanses without visual interruption. From pocketing configurations that vanish into walls to corners that open freely, the system removes physical and conceptual barriers—inviting light, air and movement to shape the space.

The sliding panels can span up to 12m² each and glide effortlessly on high-performance rollers, even at extreme dimensions. With up to four tracks and multiple configurations—standard, pocket, curved, motorised, corner or hidden rail—The Himax X Sliding Door accommodates the most dynamic layouts while maintaining a consistent architectural language. Every profile is designed with minimal expression, ensuring the glazing takes centre stage.

Every detail of the Himax X Sliding Door has been resolved to support a language of calm minimalism. From the flush handle to the invisible threshold, this system is defined not by what you see—but by what you no longer need to.

Needle-bearing rollers enable a fluid, near-frictionless glide. Wheel systems are mounted to the mullion—not beneath the glass—enabling tall, narrow doors to perform smoothly. With floor-concealed thresholds and fully integrated locking, the entire system is engineered to appear as little as possible, while doing more than ever before.

A flush-fit colour-matched handle integrates into the profile, maintaining visual continuity. Unlike brush seals that degrade over time, the Himax X Sliding Door uses durable, concealed gaskets between interlocking panels for a sleek, reliable finish. Its slim central mullion and virtually invisible frames maintain architectural intent—whether the doors are open or closed.
Exceptional thermal performance, weather resistance and long-term serviceability

The Himax X Sliding Door is a system built not only for elegance but for endurance. Its technical capability underpins its visual lightness, offering exceptional thermal performance, weather resistance and long-term serviceability—all without compromising its minimalist aesthetic.

The Himax X Sliding Door supports glass up to 36mm thick and leaf weights up to 1000kg, all while meeting rigorous performance benchmarks: thermal insulation down to 1.1 W/m²K, sound insulation up to 41dB, and class-leading ratings for air (Class 4), water (Class 9A), and wind tightness (Class C5). Its ability to handle ±2mm glass tolerance per panel ensures consistent alignment and operation across large, multi-panel installations.

Designed for longevity, all components—from locking keeps to rollers—are easily adjustable post-installation. Unlike conventional systems, The Himax X Sliding Door avoids fixed packers and fragile screw points, making it not only more durable but also more serviceable. This makes it a long-term investment in architectural clarity, comfort, and performance.
Elegance and security, integrating exclusive multi-point locking systems for enhanced protection

The Himax X Sliding Door combines elegance and security, integrating exclusive multi-point locking systems for enhanced protection without compromising on aesthetics. Each system features discreetly designed locking mechanisms, including a side lock integrated within the handle profile, as well as custom options for adjacent or alternating vents. Whether using an integrated locking system or magnetic activation, these solutions ensure smooth, effortless operation while maintaining a sleek, minimalist appearance.

For projects requiring cylinder locks on sliding openings, bespoke profile options are available to meet specific security needs. In addition to manual operation, motorised systems provide effortless handling, particularly for larger, heavier panels, ensuring both safety and convenience.
Designed to offer peace of mind without visual compromise, delivering a seamless blend of performance, comfort, and refined aesthetics, setting a new standard for secure, minimalist sliding doors.

Bring everyday life back to the ground floor
This family terrace in Stoke Newington was reconfigured under the direction of

, who led the overall brief, spatial strategy, and architectural design. The project focused on bringing everyday living back to the ground floor, improving headroom, and establishing a direct connection with the garden.

The existing layout placed a low bathroom and utility at the rear, with the kitchen above and a lightweight conservatory beyond. This resulted in limited daylight, poor rear headroom, and disconnected spaces. The architect’s brief was clear and disciplined. Reorder the plan so the kitchen and dining space sat at garden level, recover height without unnecessary bulk, and introduce light deep into the centre of the house.

With floor lowering restricted by a public sewer crossing the extension zone, the scheme relied on careful structural coordination and minimised build-ups. Himax was engaged to deliver the glazing elements, working within the architect’s strategy to bring light and openness into the plan while maintaining a restrained and robust architectural language.

A structural glass roof introduces daylight into the centre of the plan on a tight and overlooked site. With limited opportunities for side openings, overhead light was critical to improving the quality of the ground floor. The roof works in combination with exposed structure and reduced build-ups, helping recover valuable headroom while allowing light to penetrate deep into the kitchen dining space.

The glass roof supports the architect’s broader approach of restraint and clarity. It provides light where it is needed without visual clutter, contributing to a steady ceiling rhythm and a clear reading of the space from front to back.
Clean threshold between inside and out

Full-width glazing with slim bi-fold doors opens the kitchen dining space directly onto a stepped garden terrace. The doors create a clean threshold between inside and out, allowing the main living space to sit directly on the garden.

When closed, the glazing maintains a strong visual connection to the outside. When open, it allows the space to expand outward, reinforcing the architect’s intention for the ground floor to function as a single, continuous zone. Slim framing supports the quiet detailing used throughout the project.
One coherent space from front door to garden

The completed house reads as one coherent space from front door to garden. Headroom has been recovered where it matters, light is introduced precisely where it is needed, and circulation is clear and intuitive. Materials such as clay plaster, oak, and solid composite surfaces reinforce the calm, functional character set out in the original brief.

The glazing elements play a central role in supporting the architect’s overall vision, helping transform the way the house is experienced while remaining disciplined, practical, and easy to live in every day.

, the project brings together past and present in a carefully balanced composition that enhances everyday living while preserving the character and dignity of the original Lodge.

Architectural glazing refers to the use of glass in buildings for both aesthetic and functional purposes, such as windows, doors, facades, and skylights.
What types of glass are used in architectural glazing?
Common types of glass include tempered glass, laminated glass, insulated glass units (IGUs), low-emissivity (Low-E) glass, and smart glass.
What are the benefits of using Low-E glass?
Low-E glass improves energy efficiency by reducing heat transfer, blocks UV rays, enhances thermal comfort, and reduces glare.
How does insulated glass improve energy efficiency?
Insulated glass units (IGUs) consist of two or more glass panes separated by a spacer and sealed, providing better insulation and reducing heat loss or gain.
What is tempered glass and where is it used?
Tempered glass is a type of safety glass that is heat-treated to be stronger and, if broken, shatters into small, less harmful pieces. It is used in doors, windows, and other high-impact areas.
What is laminated glass and its advantages?
Laminated glass consists of two or more layers of glass bonded with a plastic interlayer. It provides enhanced safety, sound insulation, and UV protection.
How do you maintain and clean architectural glass?
Regular cleaning with non-abrasive, glass-specific cleaners and soft cloths is recommended. Avoid harsh chemicals and abrasive tools to prevent scratches and damage.
Yes, architectural glass can be customised in terms of size, shape, colour, texture, and special coatings to meet specific design and performance requirements.
What considerations should be made for glazing in high-traffic areas?
For high-traffic areas, safety and durability are crucial. Use of tempered or laminated glass, appropriate thickness, and ensuring proper installation are key considerations.
